title: Syncope — Approach
oneLiner: Transient loss of consciousness due to reduced cerebral perfusion; common ED/ward presentation; rule out cardiac and life-threatening causes before diagnosing benign.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cardiac arrhythmia (VT/VF/high-grade AV block):** single finding = **pulseless + unresponsive** — start CPR immediately; or **ECG showing wide-complex tachycardia, pauses >3 s, or heart rate <40** with hypotension.
- **Massive pulmonary embolism:** single finding = **hypotension + raised JVP + hypoxia (or new right heart strain on ECG – S1Q3T3, RBBB) with syncope**.
- **Acute massive bleed (e.g. ruptured AAA, GI haemorrhage):** single finding = **syncope + abdominal/back pain + hypotension that does not respond to fluids**.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** transient loss of consciousness (sudden onset, short duration <30 s, spontaneous recovery) with or without prodrome (lightheadedness, nausea, sweating, blurred vision).
- **Key exam findings:** lying & standing BP (drop ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic = orthostatic hypotension); heart rate and rhythm; signs of heart failure (raised JVP, lung crepitations); carotid bruits; neurological deficit (suggests seizure or stroke); rectal exam if GI bleed suspected.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Vasovagal syncope (most common):** triggers (pain, emotion, prolonged standing), prodrome of nausea/sweating, normal ECG.
- **Orthostatic hypotension:** drop in BP on standing, often due to medications (antihypertensives, diuretics), dehydration, autonomic failure.
- **Cardiac syncope (arrhythmic or structural):** abrupt onset without prodrome, occurs during exercise, family history of sudden cardiac death; ECG may show long QT, Brugada, WPW, or structural abnormality (e.g. aortic stenosis, HOCM – harsh systolic murmur).
- **Seizure (mimic):** tonic-clonic movements, post-ictal confusion, tongue bite, incontinence; may have preceding aura.
- **Psychogenic pseudosyncope:** frequent episodes with normal vitals/during emotional stress; no injury, but can appear convincing.
- **Splitter:** **ECG + prodrome** – vasovagal has prodrome; cardiac syncope often does not. Check for injury – unheralded syncope with injury is cardiac until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Immediate 12-lead ECG.
  - Lying and standing BP (after 1–5 min standing; if patient cannot stand, check supine-to-sitting).
  - Blood glucose (finger-stick) — rule out hypoglycaemia.
  - Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age) — rule out ectopic/obstetric cause.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia? bleeding?), U&E (dehydration, electrolyte abnormalities), troponin (if chest pain or suspicion of MI/arrhythmia).
  - Consider D-dimer (if low pre-test probability for PE; if high probability, proceed to CTPA).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** ECG (already done). If head injury or neurological signs → CT head.
  - **Second-line:** Echocardiogram (if structural heart disease suspected on exam/ECG); CTPA if PE suspected.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Carotid sinus massage** (only in patients >40 with unexplained syncope and no carotid bruit, ideally with monitoring).
  - **Tilt-table test** if recurrent vasovagal/orthostatic syncope unresponsive to initial management.
  - **Holter or event monitor** (if arrhythmic syncope suspected but not captured on inpatient telemetry).
- **Never-miss test:** **12-lead ECG** in all patients with syncope — omission can miss lethal arrhythmias. Also: **pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Lie patient flat, raise legs, give oxygen if hypoxic.
  - If hypotensive (systolic <90): IV crystalloid bolus (500 mL over 10–15 min, reassess).
  - Treat life-threatening arrhythmia per ALS algorithm (e.g. defibrillation for VT/VF, atropine or pacing for symptomatic bradycardia).
  - Correct reversible causes: bleeding (fluids, cross-match, surgical review), hypoglycaemia (1 ampule 50% dextrose).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Admit for telemetry if cardiac cause suspected or any of: abnormal ECG, history of heart disease, syncope during exertion, family history of sudden cardiac death.
  - Investigate for cause as per differential (echocardiogram, Holter, CTPA, etc.).
  - If orthostatic hypotension: stop/reduce offending drugs, increase oral fluid and salt intake, consider compression stockings.
  - If vasovagal: patient education on triggers, counterpressure manoeuvres (cross legs, clench fists, isometric arm exercises).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Cardiology or syncope clinic follow-up for recurrent or unexplained syncope.
  - Definitive treatment: pacemaker for heart block, ICD for VT/VF, catheter ablation for certain arrhythmias, surgery for structural heart disease.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h if hypotensive and no contra-indication (e.g. heart failure).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless on full anticoagulation).
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 4 hours; cardiac telemetry if indicated.
  - **Diet:** normal diet unless NBM for investigation (e.g. echo if stable).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **No routine drug for syncope per se** — treat underlying cause.
- **For orthostatic hypotension (if conservative measures fail):**
  - Fludrocortisone 50–100 micrograms PO once daily (off-label; start low, monitor K+, BP). Dose: [check local policy] as paediatric/elderly variations exist.
  - Midodrine 2.5–10 mg PO tds (off-label; avoid in heart failure, renal impairment). Dose: [check local policy].
- **For symptomatic bradycardia (EMERGENCY):**
  - Atropine 500 micrograms IV bolus, repeat up to 3 mg (max total dose 3 mg).
  - Isoprenaline IV infusion (1–4 mcg/min, titrate to effect) only under senior guidance.
- **Key contraidications/interactions:**
  - Fludrocortisone: avoid in heart failure, hypertension, hypokalaemia.
  - Midodrine: contra-indicated in severe hypertension, thyrotoxicosis, phaeochromocytoma.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Midodrine dose adjust in renal impairment (avoid if CrCl <25 mL/min). Fludrocortisone – caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Atropine** – giving too small a dose (<500 mcg) can cause paradoxical bradycardia; give at least 500 mcg IV bolus. Also: **midodrine** – causing supine hypertension (give last dose 4 hours before bedtime).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Injury from fall (fractures, head trauma).
  - Aspiration (if seizure or prolonged unconsciousness).
  - Rare: cardiac arrest if arrhythmic cause missed.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent falls leading to fear, immobility, institutionalisation.
  - Permanent pacemaker/ICD related complications (infection, lead issues).
sections.Prognosis:
- Vasovagal syncope: excellent; risk of death same as age-matched population.
- Orthostatic hypotension: related to underlying cause (e.g. Parkinson's disease, diabetes).
- Cardiac syncope: 1-year mortality up to 18–30% (especially structural heart disease/arrhythmia) — urgent investigation and treatment required.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always **ask about prodrome and triggers** – if typical vasovagal, reassure without over-investigating.
- **Check lying and standing BP** in every syncope patient — cheap, high-yield.
- **Get an ECG** even if patient is now asymptomatic – look for QT prolongation, Brugada pattern, pre-excitation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never discharge a syncope patient without an ECG** – misses up to 30% of lethal arrhythmias.
- **Don't assume vasovagal** if syncope occurs during exertion (e.g. running, swimming) – think HOCM, arrhythmia, or valve disease.
- **Avoid giving antiemetics (e.g. metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)** for prodromal nausea — they can cause hypotension and worsen syncope.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** any further blackouts, chest pain or palpitations, head trauma, or new neurological symptoms (e.g. weakness, speech difficulty).
- **Follow-up:** GP if vasovagal/orthostatic (no red flags); cardiology clinic if unexplained or abnormal ECG/echo.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/arrest team if:** pulseless, GCS <13, NEWS2 ≥7, hypotension unresponsive to fluids, new arrhythmia requiring ALS.
- **Call rapid response/ICU if:** lactate >4 mmol/L, need for vasopressors, unstable brady/tachyarrhythmia, or need for urgent transvenous pacing.

rawMarkdown:
## Syncope — Approach
**One-liner:** Transient loss of consciousness due to reduced cerebral perfusion; common ED/ward presentation; rule out cardiac and life-threatening causes before diagnosing benign.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cardiac arrhythmia (VT/VF/high-grade AV block):** single finding = **pulseless + unresponsive** — start CPR immediately; or **ECG showing wide-complex tachycardia, pauses >3 s, or heart rate <40** with hypotension.
- **Massive pulmonary embolism:** single finding = **hypotension + raised JVP + hypoxia (or new right heart strain on ECG – S1Q3T3, RBBB) with syncope**.
- **Acute massive bleed (e.g. ruptured AAA, GI haemorrhage):** single finding = **syncope + abdominal/back pain + hypotension that does not respond to fluids**.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** transient loss of consciousness (sudden onset, short duration <30 s, spontaneous recovery) with or without prodrome (lightheadedness, nausea, sweating, blurred vision).
- **Key exam findings:** lying & standing BP (drop ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic = orthostatic hypotension); heart rate and rhythm; signs of heart failure (raised JVP, lung crepitations); carotid bruits; neurological deficit (suggests seizure or stroke); rectal exam if GI bleed suspected.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Vasovagal syncope (most common):** triggers (pain, emotion, prolonged standing), prodrome of nausea/sweating, normal ECG.
- **Orthostatic hypotension:** drop in BP on standing, often due to medications (antihypertensives, diuretics), dehydration, autonomic failure.
- **Cardiac syncope (arrhythmic or structural):** abrupt onset without prodrome, occurs during exercise, family history of sudden cardiac death; ECG may show long QT, Brugada, WPW, or structural abnormality (e.g. aortic stenosis, HOCM – harsh systolic murmur).
- **Seizure (mimic):** tonic-clonic movements, post-ictal confusion, tongue bite, incontinence; may have preceding aura.
- **Psychogenic pseudosyncope:** frequent episodes with normal vitals/during emotional stress; no injury, but can appear convincing.
- **Splitter:** **ECG + prodrome** – vasovagal has prodrome; cardiac syncope often does not. Check for injury – unheralded syncope with injury is cardiac until proven otherwise.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Immediate 12-lead ECG.
  - Lying and standing BP (after 1–5 min standing; if patient cannot stand, check supine-to-sitting).
  - Blood glucose (finger-stick) — rule out hypoglycaemia.
  - Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age) — rule out ectopic/obstetric cause.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia? bleeding?), U&E (dehydration, electrolyte abnormalities), troponin (if chest pain or suspicion of MI/arrhythmia).
  - Consider D-dimer (if low pre-test probability for PE; if high probability, proceed to CTPA).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** ECG (already done). If head injury or neurological signs → CT head.
  - **Second-line:** Echocardiogram (if structural heart disease suspected on exam/ECG); CTPA if PE suspected.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Carotid sinus massage** (only in patients >40 with unexplained syncope and no carotid bruit, ideally with monitoring).
  - **Tilt-table test** if recurrent vasovagal/orthostatic syncope unresponsive to initial management.
  - **Holter or event monitor** (if arrhythmic syncope suspected but not captured on inpatient telemetry).
- **Never-miss test:** **12-lead ECG** in all patients with syncope — omission can miss lethal arrhythmias. Also: **pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Lie patient flat, raise legs, give oxygen if hypoxic.
  - If hypotensive (systolic <90): IV crystalloid bolus (500 mL over 10–15 min, reassess).
  - Treat life-threatening arrhythmia per ALS algorithm (e.g. defibrillation for VT/VF, atropine or pacing for symptomatic bradycardia).
  - Correct reversible causes: bleeding (fluids, cross-match, surgical review), hypoglycaemia (1 ampule 50% dextrose).
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Admit for telemetry if cardiac cause suspected or any of: abnormal ECG, history of heart disease, syncope during exertion, family history of sudden cardiac death.
  - Investigate for cause as per differential (echocardiogram, Holter, CTPA, etc.).
  - If orthostatic hypotension: stop/reduce offending drugs, increase oral fluid and salt intake, consider compression stockings.
  - If vasovagal: patient education on triggers, counterpressure manoeuvres (cross legs, clench fists, isometric arm exercises).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Cardiology or syncope clinic follow-up for recurrent or unexplained syncope.
  - Definitive treatment: pacemaker for heart block, ICD for VT/VF, catheter ablation for certain arrhythmias, surgery for structural heart disease.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h if hypotensive and no contra-indication (e.g. heart failure).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless on full anticoagulation).
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 4 hours; cardiac telemetry if indicated.
  - **Diet:** normal diet unless NBM for investigation (e.g. echo if stable).

### Drugs & Doses
- **No routine drug for syncope per se** — treat underlying cause.
- **For orthostatic hypotension (if conservative measures fail):**
  - Fludrocortisone 50–100 micrograms PO once daily (off-label; start low, monitor K+, BP). Dose: [check local policy] as paediatric/elderly variations exist.
  - Midodrine 2.5–10 mg PO tds (off-label; avoid in heart failure, renal impairment). Dose: [check local policy].
- **For symptomatic bradycardia (EMERGENCY):**
  - Atropine 500 micrograms IV bolus, repeat up to 3 mg (max total dose 3 mg).
  - Isoprenaline IV infusion (1–4 mcg/min, titrate to effect) only under senior guidance.
- **Key contraidications/interactions:**
  - Fludrocortisone: avoid in heart failure, hypertension, hypokalaemia.
  - Midodrine: contra-indicated in severe hypertension, thyrotoxicosis, phaeochromocytoma.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Midodrine dose adjust in renal impairment (avoid if CrCl <25 mL/min). Fludrocortisone – caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Atropine** – giving too small a dose (<500 mcg) can cause paradoxical bradycardia; give at least 500 mcg IV bolus. Also: **midodrine** – causing supine hypertension (give last dose 4 hours before bedtime).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Injury from fall (fractures, head trauma).
  - Aspiration (if seizure or prolonged unconsciousness).
  - Rare: cardiac arrest if arrhythmic cause missed.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent falls leading to fear, immobility, institutionalisation.
  - Permanent pacemaker/ICD related complications (infection, lead issues).

### Prognosis
- Vasovagal syncope: excellent; risk of death same as age-matched population.
- Orthostatic hypotension: related to underlying cause (e.g. Parkinson's disease, diabetes).
- Cardiac syncope: 1-year mortality up to 18–30% (especially structural heart disease/arrhythmia) — urgent investigation and treatment required.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always **ask about prodrome and triggers** – if typical vasovagal, reassure without over-investigating.
- **Check lying and standing BP** in every syncope patient — cheap, high-yield.
- **Get an ECG** even if patient is now asymptomatic – look for QT prolongation, Brugada pattern, pre-excitation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never discharge a syncope patient without an ECG** – misses up to 30% of lethal arrhythmias.
- **Don't assume vasovagal** if syncope occurs during exertion (e.g. running, swimming) – think HOCM, arrhythmia, or valve disease.
- **Avoid giving antiemetics (e.g. metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)** for prodromal nausea — they can cause hypotension and worsen syncope.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** any further blackouts, chest pain or palpitations, head trauma, or new neurological symptoms (e.g. weakness, speech difficulty).
- **Follow-up:** GP if vasovagal/orthostatic (no red flags); cardiology clinic if unexplained or abnormal ECG/echo.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/arrest team if:** pulseless, GCS <13, NEWS2 ≥7, hypotension unresponsive to fluids, new arrhythmia requiring ALS.
- **Call rapid response/ICU if:** lactate >4 mmol/L, need for vasopressors, unstable brady/tachyarrhythmia, or need for urgent transvenous pacing.
